sentences.text: I have it in dreams.
You wouldn't believe what dreams I had.
We read in paper that it's very bad in Poland.
And one time I had a deem that I was living by the Volga.
I had a dream that the train is coming from Poland through the Volga.
In the train will be my husband's mother and his sister.
And I put on long shoes.
And I'm running to Volga.
And I'm running in Volga, and I saw the train.
And I catched her hand-- his sister, her name was Manya and I catched her, and I said, where are you going?
And said, don't ask me where I am going.
Here is-- I know, she said, that you are sewing a black blouse.
Here is a piece of green material.
Make from the green material a collar to the black.
It will be a little lively.
And I said to her, tell me, where are you going?
We are going to die.
And she ran away.
The train ran away, and I am walking with Volga, going back.
And I was crying.
I will never forget this dream.
There, a few months later, we find out what is going on, how they killed people.
Then I had other-- I had in dreams this what was going on in Poland.
Oh Another dream with horses.
They put on horses, and each horse has a line-- a wire.
In every wire was a Jew put together.
And then they give a-- a command for the horses to run.
And all the people were killed by the horses.
You cannot forget those dreams.
It was terrible.
I couldn't sleep at night.
I was crying.
My husband used to wake me up-- what are you-- what are you hollering?
Lots of dreams.
I saw everything in dreams.
Did you ever meet any refugees who came to Birsk who told you what was happening?
From the Russian?
No, we didn't see, in Birsk, nobody.
When we came back, there are papers.
And we gave ads in papers-- we are looking for this person and this person.
And I was looking in the papers, and I found in a paper a ad, Bella Szpinak with this-- with her mother from Warsaw is looking for friends and relatives-- it's his aunt and his cousin.
And there is a address to go to the Jewish committee in Warsaw with the address.
I came there, and I asked for them.
And they said, we don't have on the list with that-- by this name.
And I showed them the paper-- find out in Jewish committee.
And they look.
They cannot find it.
And I sit there, and I holler, what is it?
You have the paper.
You have to look.
Somewhere she is there.
Finally, they found that she is in-- in a little city not far from Warsaw.
I will recall it, the number.
And I'm going in a train.
This was after the pogrom in Kielce.
And I'm going there.
And in the train was sitting a little-- a lady.
And I asked her, do you-- are you from the city?
She said yes.
I am looking in the city for my aunt and cousin, which supposed to be there, and I haven't got where to stay.
And she said, you can stay with us.
She took me to her house.
And they were very nice to me, very good.
It was on a Saturday.
She said, today, the offices are closed.
Tomorrow-- it was Sunday-- tomorrow we will find out.
We went to [INAUDIBLE] to test the chief of the little city, Piastow-- the city was Piastow.
We will ask him about the address.
There is a address, but the address is in a church.
And I asked the lady, do you know that some people were living here?
She said, maybe in the time of war, maybe they were hiding here.
The number is right.
I have the number, but nobody's there with the name.
And I decided to write-- to go back to Warsaw.

And I wrote a ad, and I hanged it on the pharmacy and this stop-- bus stops, where is a bigger communication, Stella Faitek and Israel Faitek are looking for those and those, my address and telephone.
I came back to Warsaw, and I told them there is nobody.
They were hidden there in time of war.
And I came back, and they told me another address.
I came back again, I looked, nobody's there.
Going back to Warsaw, I saw my girlfriend, which was hidden in ghetto.
And I was  in Russia.
And I'm very happy to see her.
And I said to her, you know what, will you please be so kind, take this ad.
Go to the paper.
And give the ad  in paper, because I left my child home alone, and I have to rush home.
She said OK.
She took the ad.
She went to the office there.
She gave the ad to them.
There was my cousin working there.
She took the ad.
She recognized her, but she is playing-- working there as a Polish woman.
She cannot tell her that she knows her.
She takes the address.
What life is going just like this-- if I would go, I would find her right away.
But since she had the ad-- the ad in hand, with the telephone, I came back to Lodz.
From Lodz, I came to Warsaw.
I came there-- back to Lodz.
There is a telephone from her.
So I found her.
Then I found a few more people through the paper.
People were looking for each other.
Then I was looking for my brother, and I found my brother.
Joseph, the older one.
Sam was with me, yeah.
He was working in Syktyvkar in the coal mine.
He survived after the war.
He came there, and he was in the German city, which was belonging later to Poland, Rychbach-- Reichenbach.
And I came there, and I found him there.
Yeah, yeah.
He was in coal mine, working.
I told you that he was with his cousin, walking to Russian border.
And he survived through the concentration camp there.
He was working all the time in coal mine.
Terrible.
When the wagon crossed the border, there were lots of Polish people with stones and knocking on the wall.
So many Jews still less-- left?
So many Jews?
You go back.
It was terrible.
Yeah, coming back.
Coming back to Poland.
It was a terrible experience.
And then we-- when we were in Poland, we had the Kielce pogrom and demonstrations on the street against the Jews.
But we weren't long in Poland.
We went out to Germany.
For sure.
Sure.
When we came back, we were very afraid.
I was afraid to go out at night.
But we weren't long.
The Kielce pogroms, just like now I see it.
On the streets, very many-- yeah, I went to buy, uh, pillowcases.
And the Polish woman was standing with the material-- it was like a bazaar here.
And I asked her, with my husband I am staying there, how much it is.
She said 2,000 zlotys. 2,000 zlotys was a lot of money.
And I said, no, that's too much for me.
And she looked at me, and my husband went away.
She said, how come that you married a Jew?
And I said, I'll tell you why.
Uh.
How come that you didn't-- what was it?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somehow it came out that she asked me how come that you-- you are not married to a Jew.
And I said I was married to a Jew.
I married a Jew.
I had my first-- oh, I remember now.
I marry a Jew, because my first husband was a Polack, and he was beating me up.
He was drinking.
And now I have a Jewish husband.
I know a life.
He is very good to me, because I [? paid ?] [? her ?] [? back. ?] [LAUGHS] There was an incident on a train where you were in danger, and instant-- an instant where something almost fell on you?
Oh, yeah.
This was a time when I was going to my brother, to Reichenbach.
And we were sitting in train.
My son was by the window.
In Poland, going from Lodz to Rychbach.
In the other side from the window was sitting a woman.
And I was sitting at the edge, and here a woman at the edge.
And the woman-- no-- where we were sitting there, a woman came in..
And she pointed out who is Jewish.
They took out from the train three Jews.
They never came-- it was a stop.
They never came back.
The woman, with two policemen, came back.
And she stopped by me, and she said, I think that you are Jewish, too.
But here, I play with life.
I said, I think that you are Jewish.
My Polish language is beautiful.
And she was from a little city, from-- far from Warsaw, from big city.
And she said, what do we have to argue?
She has a son here.
This was a miracle.
My son wasn't circumcised in Russia.
They let the pants down.
He wasn't circumcised.
And she said, well, maybe she's not Jewish.
A few minutes later, the lady across from my son by the window said-- asked me, will you please switch with me?
I cannot sit by the window.
It runs so fast I have a headache.
I said OK.
I switched with her.
A few minutes later, a head from a sewing machine fells on her, and she was killed.
Do you believe in destiny?
This was my destiny.
Twice in one-- in a few hours.
Eventually, you went to Germany.
And you first came to a-- a-- a-- a DP camp, a displaced persons camp in Ulm.
Ulm, yeah.
It was '46.
We were there until '48? '48, they send us to-- uh-- out from Ulm.
Fohrenwald.
Yeah, in Ulm.
And from there, we went to Fohrenwald.
In Fohrenwald, we went until-- Mark was born in 1950.
In '51, we went out from Fohrenwald to America.
It was just the same, like it was in Ulm.
This is Fohrenwald.
Oh, you ask about Ulm?
We were in [NON-ENGLISH].
No, it was-- before we came there, it was for military.
And there on the street were apartments.
We had our apartment by the big place.
And everybody got their rations from the [NON-ENGLISH].
We lived very comfortable.
We had everything.
It was quiet.
From Ulm?
I want to go to Canada, because my cousins were there.
But we went through a commission.
And my son had spina bifida, little bone on the back.
And he was inter-- he was looked by a doctor, and he had a hand there.
And when he slid the hand down, he felt the bone.
He said to me, why didn't you tell me that he has tuberculosis, bone tuberculosis?
I said, it's not bone tuberculosis.
He has spina bifida.
That's a lie.
And he wrote that I lied.
And they didn't accept me to go to Canada.
Then we wrote to my cousin.
And my cousin said, I will try to get you there.
In the meantime, there's a registration to go to America.
So I registered to go to America, because they throw me out, and I don't know what will be farther.
Then I got a letter.
When I was registered to go to America, I got a note that I will go to a commission to go to America.
I got a letter from my cousin, that she had a rich man, a millionaire, which guaranteed for us with $2 million to come to Canada.
He will guarantee for everything, and you are coming to us.
So in the meantime, we were called to the-- to go to America, so we went to America.
And that's the way we are here.
No.
To go to Israel, I think-- thought about going when I was in Germany.
But at that time, there was no way to go, because Israel wasn't free.
Israel was under the English.
Oh, in Poland, before the war?
To the [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
After the war, Israel wasn't Israel.
Israel was Palestine, [INTERPOSING VOICES] so we couldn't think about it.
And when Israel was free, when we could go, I felt very comfortable in America.
But I went later on.
I went to see Israel.
I had lots of friends.
I had relatives there.
Yeah, the same thing.
The same thing.
[INAUDIBLE].
It was-- Later it wasn't [INAUDIBLE].
Later it was another name.
But we got support from [?
Joint, ?] too.
But this was something extra.
But everything, we got from [INAUDIBLE].
The food was very good.
Everything was fine.
And Mark was born in time when I was  in Fohrenwald.
Mark was born in Munich, 1950.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
My brother used to go out with a lady.
Her husband was killed in the army.
Was a very nice lady, a very fine lady.
I used to correspond with her, about a year after I came back to Poland.
Later, I didn't write to her anymore-- was a nice person.
From-- from Russia, we went back to Poland.
Yeah.
I went back just for a visit, not to stay there.
I was twice back to Poland, because I have two-- have a cousin there.
Though in the second time-- the first time, I came to a conference, [INAUDIBLE] conference.
All the children which are alive, the personnel which was working there, were meeting in Warsaw.
There I was four weeks.
It was about eight, nine years ago.
And then four years ago, I went back to Poland to see my cousins.
And from that time, I never went back.
Why?
I didn't come-- I wanted to go-- come to Chicago, but we were marked to go to-- not to Chicago, but to Atlanta, Georgia.
My husband's a tailor.
I had an uncle in Chicago, and I want to go to Chicago.
So when we came to New York, it was on a Friday.
And it was before the holidays.
And I decided to go to the [?
Joint ?] to ask to send me to Chicago, because I have an uncle here, and it's closer to Toronto.
My brother was in Toronto.
And they gave me the permission to go to Toron-- to Chicago.
But otherwise, I would be in Atlanta, because if you are chosen to go to America, they gave you a place where to go.
You don't choose where do you want to go.
But when I was here, I told them that I have a sister here.
And I lied.
It's not a-- not a sister.
They came before us.
It was a neighbor.
And they checked that she is here, so they let me go to-- to Chicago.
It was strange.
It was dirty.
It was wind blow and all the papers and plastic on my face.
And if you get-- got such a dirty-- beautiful city, dirty, flies everything.
But I got used to it.
[LAUGHS] The people were very friendly, very nice.
And--
We went to hotel.
We stayed in a hotel.
And then my husband was looking for work.
He start working.
I had here a uncle, a aunt, two cousins.
They came to see me in hotel.
And, uh, we saw them from time to time.
When I was settled in our apartment, I made a birthday party for the children.
They were invited.
We were a short time very close, but my uncle died.
And then my aunt died, so it's a different life for me with my cousins.
They are different than I am.
In what way?
They consider me lower from what they are.
They called me [INAUDIBLE].
So-- and, uh, they think that they are higher.
They asked me beginning, did you have a telephone?
Did you have a washroom?
Things which are unbelievable.
I said, we had a telephone.
We had washrooms.
We had televisions.
Televisions too?
Yes, we had televisions.
Then she-- I asked her to give me the telephone number to call her.
And I write it.
And she said to her husband, take a look, [?
Sydney, ?] her beautiful handwriting.
I said, what do you think, that we are coming from the woods?
They-- they are different from us.
Then, a time later, my uncle died.
And he was in Drexel Home.
Here is a home, Drexel.
And she called me, if she can-- before he went to Drexel Home, she asked me if I want to take care of the uncle.
And I said, how can I take care of your uncle-- of your father when I have two children, and I have two bedrooms?
So they located him in Drexel Home.
I used to come once a week there.
He liked kreplach.
He liked gefilte fish.
I used to bring it to him.
And one day, I came, and my uncle died the same day.
And they called the daughter.
She came, and from that time on, we are not close anymore.
Right.
You said it.
And a sick husband.
With ulcer.
Yeah.
He worked in-- here in Chicago as a furrier.
And we made a living.
Sure.
My children were at school, special school.
They were very good students until they grew up.
Leon worked.
And Mark was working, little by little, so we managed.
I think it's harder to raise your own children than childrens-- strange children.
It's very hard, especially in the case of hemophilia.
You have to be very careful.
You have to be strict with them-- don't do this, don't do this, don't do this.
I knew, and I felt that sometimes, they hate me.
But I hadn't got a choice.
One time, Leon said to me, ma, I know that you are right, but I cannot take all the time no, no, no.
I will not listen to you.
Even you are right, I will not listen.
Mark was at that time four years old, and he want to play baseball.
He want to do-- to go on a bicycle.
I said, you cannot go play ball, because you have to run, and you might fell-- fell.
You can't go on a bicycle, because you have to drive with your legs, but you have very often swollen legs.
But I took them to play tennis, ping pong.
I bought that table.
We played ping pong, tennis.
But they want something else.
They had something in sport in themself from me.
They want to do things.
So I knew, and I felt that they hate me sometimes.
It's hard to raise children, your own.
But in a case like this, it's very, very hard.
Ulcers.
And he played the role that he is always sick, never helped me.
But he was a sick person.
Well, I adjust to everything.
Everything is easier for me.
I know that Leon has a marvelous wife.
Mark has a wife.
So my responsibilities not so much.
I enjoy it now, life.
I enjoy life.
And I try to give something from my life.
I volunteer 12 years in a old home, Northwest Home.
I work there three times a week, three hours every time.
And I am satisfied that I can help.
This gives me a satisfaction.
Oh, it's by volunteering, you get a lot of satis-- you can get a lot of satisfaction.
Right.
Right.
I know that I help somebody.
OK, I know that we're--
OK.
